[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6d/Northern_and_Southern_Ireland.png/250px-Northern_and_Southern_Ireland.png)](/wiki/File:Northern_and_Southern_Ireland.png)

Northern and Southern Ireland

The **Government of Ireland Act 1920** ([10 & 11 Geo. 5](/wiki/10_%26_11_Geo._5 "10 & 11 Geo. 5"). c. 67) was an [act](/wiki/Act_of_Parliament "Act of Parliament") of the [Parliament of the United Kingdom](/wiki/Parliament_of_the_United_Kingdom "Parliament of the United Kingdom"). The Act's [long title](/wiki/Long_title "Long title") was "An Act to provide for the better government of Ireland"; it is also known as the **Fourth Home Rule Bill** or (inaccurately) as the **Fourth Home Rule Act** and informally known as the **Partition Act**.[[3]](#cite_note-3) The Act was intended to [partition Ireland](/wiki/Partition_of_Ireland "Partition of Ireland") into two [self-governing](/wiki/Home_rule "Home rule") polities: the six north-eastern counties were to form "Northern Ireland", while the larger part of the country was to form "[Southern Ireland](/wiki/Southern_Ireland_(1921%E2%80%931922) "Southern Ireland (1921–1922)")".[[4]](#cite_note-4) Both territories were to remain part of the [United Kingdom of Great Britain and Ireland](/wiki/United_Kingdom_of_Great_Britain_and_Ireland "United Kingdom of Great Britain and Ireland") and provision was made for their future reunification through a [Council of Ireland](/wiki/Council_of_Ireland "Council of Ireland"). The Act was passed by the British Parliament in November 1920, received [royal assent](/wiki/Royal_assent "Royal assent") in December and came into force on 3 May 1921.[[5]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEO'Day1998299-5)[[6]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJackson2004368–370-6)

The smaller Northern Ireland was duly created with a [devolved](/wiki/Devolved "Devolved") [government](/wiki/Government_of_Northern_Ireland_(1921%E2%80%931972) "Government of Northern Ireland (1921–1972)") and remained in the UK.[[7]](#cite_note-7) The larger Southern Ireland was not recognised by most of its citizens, who instead recognised the self-declared [Irish Republic](/wiki/Irish_Republic "Irish Republic") in the ongoing [Irish War of Independence](/wiki/Irish_War_of_Independence "Irish War of Independence"). The conflict resulted in the [Anglo-Irish Treaty](/wiki/Anglo-Irish_Treaty "Anglo-Irish Treaty") in December 1921. Under the treaty, Ireland would leave the UK (with the option for Northern Ireland to opt out and remain in the UK, which it immediately did) in December 1922 and become the [Irish Free State](/wiki/Irish_Free_State "Irish Free State"), which would later evolve into today's Republic of Ireland. The institutions set up under this Act for Northern Ireland continued to function until they were [suspended by the British parliament in 1972](/wiki/Northern_Ireland_(Temporary_Provisions)_Act_1972 "Northern Ireland (Temporary Provisions) Act 1972") as a consequence of [the Troubles](/wiki/The_Troubles "The Troubles").

The remaining provisions of the Act still in force in Northern Ireland were repealed under the terms of the 1998 [Good Friday Agreement](/wiki/Good_Friday_Agreement "Good Friday Agreement").

## Background

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Government_of_Ireland_Act_1920&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Background")]

Main article: [Irish Home Rule movement](/wiki/Irish_Home_Rule_movement "Irish Home Rule movement")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e8/David_Lloyd_George.jpg/250px-David_Lloyd_George.jpg)](/wiki/File:David_Lloyd_George.jpg)

David Lloyd George, MP. The [British Prime Minister](/wiki/British_Prime_Minister "British Prime Minister") was the author of the new Act.

Various attempts had been made to give Ireland limited regional [self-government](/wiki/Devolution_in_the_United_Kingdom "Devolution in the United Kingdom"), known as [Home rule](/wiki/Home_rule "Home rule"), in the late 19th and early 20th centuries.

The [First Home Rule Bill](/wiki/First_Home_Rule_Bill "First Home Rule Bill") of 1886 was defeated in the [House of Commons](/wiki/House_of_Commons_of_the_United_Kingdom "House of Commons of the United Kingdom") because of [a split in the Liberal Party](/wiki/Liberal_Party_(UK)#Gladstone_era "Liberal Party (UK)") over the principle of Home Rule, while the [Second Home Rule Bill](/wiki/Second_Home_Rule_Bill "Second Home Rule Bill") of 1893, having been passed by the Commons was vetoed by the [House of Lords](/wiki/House_of_Lords "House of Lords"). The [Third Home Rule Bill](/wiki/Third_Home_Rule_Bill "Third Home Rule Bill") introduced in 1912 by the [Irish Parliamentary Party](/wiki/Irish_Parliamentary_Party "Irish Parliamentary Party") could no longer be vetoed after the passing of the [Parliament Act 1911](/wiki/Parliament_Act_1911 "Parliament Act 1911") which removed the power of the Lords to veto bills. They could merely be delayed for two years.

Because of the continuing threat of civil war in Ireland, King [George V](/wiki/George_V "George V") called the [Buckingham Palace Conference](/wiki/Buckingham_Palace_Conference "Buckingham Palace Conference") in July 1914 where Irish Nationalist and Unionist leaders failed to reach agreement.[[8]](#cite_note-8) Controversy continued over the rival demands of Irish Nationalists, backed by the Liberals (for all-Ireland home rule), and Irish Unionists, backed by the Conservatives, for the exclusion of most or all of the province of [Ulster](/wiki/Ulster "Ulster").[[9]](#cite_note-9) In an attempt at compromise, the British government put forward an amending bill, which would have allowed for Ulster to be temporarily excluded from the working of the act; this failed to satisfy either side and the stalemate continued until overtaken by the outbreak of [World War I](/wiki/World_War_I "World War I"). A few weeks after the British entry into the war, the act received [royal assent](/wiki/Royal_assent "Royal assent"), while the amending bill was abandoned. However, the [Suspensory Act 1914](/wiki/Suspensory_Act_1914 "Suspensory Act 1914") (which received royal assent on the same day) meant that implementation would be suspended for the duration of what was expected to be only a short European war.

## Developments in Ireland

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Government_of_Ireland_Act_1920&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Developments in Ireland")]

During the Great War, Irish politics moved decisively in a different direction. Several events, including the [Easter Rising](/wiki/Easter_Rising "Easter Rising") of 1916, the subsequent reaction of the British Government and the [Conscription Crisis of 1918](/wiki/Conscription_Crisis_of_1918 "Conscription Crisis of 1918"), had altered the state of Irish politics and contributed to [Sinn Féin](/wiki/Sinn_F%C3%A9in "Sinn Féin") becoming the dominant voice of [Irish nationalism](/wiki/Irish_nationalism "Irish nationalism"). Sinn Féin, standing for 'an independent sovereign Ireland', won 73 of the 105 parliamentary seats on the island in the [1918 general election](/wiki/1918_United_Kingdom_general_election "1918 United Kingdom general election").[[10]](#cite_note-10) The Sinn Féin elected members refused to take their seats in the British Parliament and therefore did not vote on the Government of Ireland Act 1920. They established their own parliament ([Dáil Éireann](/wiki/D%C3%A1il_%C3%89ireann_(Irish_Republic) "Dáil Éireann (Irish Republic)")), which [declared the country's independence](/wiki/Irish_Declaration_of_Independence "Irish Declaration of Independence") as the [Irish Republic](/wiki/Irish_Republic "Irish Republic"). Dáil Éireann, after a number of meetings, was declared illegal in September 1919 by the [Lord Lieutenant of Ireland](/wiki/Lord_Lieutenant_of_Ireland "Lord Lieutenant of Ireland"), [Viscount French](/wiki/John_French,_1st_Earl_of_Ypres "John French, 1st Earl of Ypres").

When the Act became law on 23 December 1920 it was already out of touch with realities in Ireland. The long-standing demand for home rule had been replaced among nationalists by a demand for complete independence (an [Irish Republic](/wiki/Irish_Republic "Irish Republic")).[[11]](#cite_note-11) The Republic's [army](/wiki/Irish_Republican_Army "Irish Republican Army") was waging the [Irish War of Independence](/wiki/Irish_War_of_Independence "Irish War of Independence") against British rule, which had reached a nadir in late 1920.

## Long's committee and bill approval

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Government_of_Ireland_Act_1920&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Long's committee and bill approval")]

Main article: [Home Rule Crisis](/wiki/Home_Rule_Crisis "Home Rule Crisis")

A delay ensued because of the effective end of the [First World War](/wiki/First_World_War "First World War") in November 1918, the [Paris Peace Conference, 1919](/wiki/Paris_Peace_Conference,_1919 "Paris Peace Conference, 1919") and the [Treaty of Versailles](/wiki/Treaty_of_Versailles "Treaty of Versailles") that was signed in June 1919. Starting in September 1919, with the British Government, now led by [David Lloyd George](/wiki/David_Lloyd_George "David Lloyd George"), committed under all circumstances to implementing Home Rule, the British cabinet's Committee for Ireland, under the chairmanship of former [Irish Unionist Alliance](/wiki/Irish_Unionist_Alliance "Irish Unionist Alliance") leader [Walter Long](/wiki/Walter_Long,_1st_Viscount_Long "Walter Long, 1st Viscount Long"), pushed for a radical new solution. Long proposed the creation of two Irish home rule entities, Northern Ireland and [Southern Ireland](/wiki/Southern_Ireland_(1921%E2%80%931922) "Southern Ireland (1921–1922)"), with separate parliaments.[[12]](#cite_note-12) The House of Lords accordingly amended the old bill to create a new bill which provided for two [bicameral](/wiki/Bicameral "Bicameral") parliaments instead of [unicameral](/wiki/Unicameral "Unicameral") parliaments, "consisting of His Majesty, the Senate of (Northern or Southern) Ireland, and the House of Commons of (Northern or Southern) Ireland."

The bill's second reading debates in late March 1920 revealed that already a large number of Irish MPs present felt that the proposals were unworkable.[[13]](#cite_note-13)[[14]](#cite_note-14)

For a variety of reasons all the Ulster Unionist MPs at Westminster voted against the Act. They preferred that all or most of Ulster would remain fully within the United Kingdom, accepting the proposed northern Home Rule state only as the second best option. The Long Committee originally called for all nine Counties of Ulster to be included in Northern Ireland. When it became clear that three counties of Ulster would be excluded from Northern Ireland, southern Unionists left the [Irish Unionist Alliance](/wiki/Irish_Unionist_Alliance "Irish Unionist Alliance") (dissolved in 1922) and formed the [Unionist Anti-Partition League](/wiki/Unionist_Anti-Partition_League "Unionist Anti-Partition League") in opposition to the impending partition of Ireland.[[15]](#cite_note-15)

After considerable delays in debating the financial aspects of the measure, the substantive third reading of the bill was approved by a large majority on 11 November 1920. On that date, [Thomas Harbison](/wiki/Thomas_Harbison "Thomas Harbison"), MP for [North East Tyrone](/wiki/North_East_Tyrone "North East Tyrone"), predicted that violence would result in his constituency:[[16]](#cite_note-16)

> "This is not a Bill for the better government of Ireland. I believe that the people in the county that I represent would be legally justified in using every form of resistance in their power to prevent this Act, if it ever becomes an Act, from coming into operation. It is a sentence of death, in my opinion, upon us as a unit in that Parliament. Our liberties are gone; and if the younger men of Ireland become indignant, and take courses that no sane man could defend, who will be responsible? The responsibility will be upon the men who have produced this Bill at the dictates of a narrow-minded set of reactionaries in the North-East corner of Ulster. It is a very small corner of Ulster; I have the map of it here. A set of reactionaries in that corner will have us under their heel for all time. I know the feeling of the men whom I represent, and I assure you, on this Armistice night, when all should be peace, that you are going to create, not peace, but eternal dissatisfaction, division, and, I am afraid, destruction."

A considerable number of the Irish Members present voted against the bill, including [Southern Unionists](/wiki/Irish_Unionist_Alliance#Southern_Unionists "Irish Unionist Alliance") such as [Maurice Dockrell](/wiki/Maurice_Dockrell_(Unionist_politician) "Maurice Dockrell (Unionist politician)") and members of the [Irish Parliamentary Party](/wiki/Irish_Parliamentary_Party "Irish Parliamentary Party") like [Joseph Devlin](/wiki/Joseph_Devlin "Joseph Devlin").[[17]](#cite_note-17) Most Irish MPs were abstaining from Westminster, sitting in Dáil Éireann.

## Two 'Home Rule' Irelands

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Government_of_Ireland_Act_1920&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Two 'Home Rule' Irelands")]

The act [divided Ireland into two territories](/wiki/Partition_of_Ireland "Partition of Ireland"), [Southern Ireland](/wiki/Southern_Ireland_(1921%E2%80%931922) "Southern Ireland (1921–1922)") and Northern Ireland, each intended to be self-governing, except in areas specifically reserved to the [Parliament of the United Kingdom](/wiki/Parliament_of_the_United_Kingdom "Parliament of the United Kingdom"): chief amongst these were matters relating to the Crown, to defence, foreign affairs, international trade and currency. Speaking in [Caernarvon](/wiki/Caernarfon "Caernarfon") in October 1920, Lloyd George stated his reasoning for reserving specific governmental functions: "The Irish temper is an uncertainty and dangerous forces like armies and navies are better under the control of the Imperial Parliament."[[18]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETownshend197536-18)

Northern Ireland was defined as "the parliamentary counties of [Antrim](/wiki/County_Antrim "County Antrim"), [Armagh](/wiki/County_Armagh "County Armagh"), [Down](/wiki/County_Down "County Down"), [Fermanagh](/wiki/County_Fermanagh "County Fermanagh"), [Londonderry](/wiki/County_Londonderry "County Londonderry") and [Tyrone](/wiki/County_Tyrone "County Tyrone"), and the parliamentary boroughs of [Belfast](/wiki/Belfast "Belfast") and [Londonderry](/wiki/Londonderry_(parliamentary_borough) "Londonderry (parliamentary borough)")", and Southern Ireland was defined as "so much of Ireland as is not comprised within the said parliamentary counties and boroughs".[[19]](#cite_note-19) Northern Ireland, amounting to six of the nine counties of [Ulster](/wiki/Ulster "Ulster"), was seen as the maximum area within which [Unionists](/wiki/Unionists_(Ireland) "Unionists (Ireland)") could be expected to have a safe majority. However, at the [1918 general election](/wiki/1918_United_Kingdom_general_election_in_Ireland "1918 United Kingdom general election in Ireland"), counties [Fermanagh](/wiki/County_Fermanagh "County Fermanagh") and [Tyrone](/wiki/County_Tyrone "County Tyrone") had Irish nationalist (Sinn Féin/[Irish Parliamentary Party](/wiki/Irish_Parliamentary_Party "Irish Parliamentary Party")) majorities.[[20]](#cite_note-20) On 14 December 1921, Lloyd George spoke of the position of Tyrone and Fermanagh:

> There is no doubt—certainly since the Act of 1920—that the majority of the people of two counties prefer being with their Southern neighbours to being in the Northern Parliament... What does that mean? If Ulster is to remain a separate community, you can only by means of coercion keep them there... Apart from that, would it be an advantage to Ulster? There is no doubt it would give her trouble. It would be a trouble at her own door, a trouble which would complicate the whole of her machinery, and take away her mind from building. She wants to construct; she wants to build up a good Government, a model Government, and she cannot do so as long as she has got a trouble like this on her own threshold, nay, inside her door.[[21]](#cite_note-21)

## Structures of the governmental system

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Government_of_Ireland_Act_1920&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Structures of the governmental system")]

At the apex of the governmental system was to be the [Lord Lieutenant of Ireland](/wiki/Lord_Lieutenant_of_Ireland "Lord Lieutenant of Ireland"), who would be the monarch's representative in both of the Irish home rule regions. The system was based on colonial constitutional theories. [Executive authority](/wiki/Executive_authority "Executive authority") was to be vested in the crown and, in theory, not answerable to either parliament. The Lord Lieutenant would appoint a cabinet that did not need parliamentary support. No provision existed for a prime minister.

Such structures matched the theory in the [British dominions](/wiki/British_dominions "British dominions"), such as Canada and Australia, wherein powers belonged to the [governor-general](/wiki/Governor-general "Governor-general") and there was no normal responsibility to parliament. In reality, governments had long come to be chosen from parliament and to be answerable to it. Prime ministerial offices had come into *de facto* existence.[[a]](#cite_note-22) Such developments were also expected to happen in Northern Ireland and Southern Ireland, but technically were not required under the act.

## Constituency reform

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Government_of_Ireland_Act_1920&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Constituency reform")]

Main article: [Government of Ireland Act 1920 (constituencies)](/wiki/Government_of_Ireland_Act_1920_(constituencies) "Government of Ireland Act 1920 (constituencies)")

The Act revised the constituencies in the [Redistribution of Seats (Ireland) Act 1918](/wiki/Redistribution_of_Seats_(Ireland)_Act_1918 "Redistribution of Seats (Ireland) Act 1918") for elections to the [Parliament of the United Kingdom](/wiki/Parliament_of_the_United_Kingdom "Parliament of the United Kingdom"). In place of the 105 [UK MPs](/wiki/Member_of_Parliament_(United_Kingdom) "Member of Parliament (United Kingdom)") for all of Ireland in 1918, there were to be 33 UK MPs from Southern Ireland and 13 UK MPs from Northern Ireland. The same constituencies were to elect 128 MPs to the Southern Ireland House of Commons and 52 MPs to the Northern Ireland House of Commons. [Elections for the two Irish parliaments](/wiki/1921_Northern_Ireland_and_Southern_Ireland_general_elections "1921 Northern Ireland and Southern Ireland general elections") took place in May 1921. No elections were held in Southern Ireland to the United Kingdom House of Commons as the [Irish Free State](/wiki/Irish_Free_State "Irish Free State") was due to be established on 6 December 1922, less than a month after the [1922 United Kingdom general election](/wiki/1922_United_Kingdom_general_election "1922 United Kingdom general election").

## Potential for Irish unity

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Government_of_Ireland_Act_1920&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Potential for Irish unity")]

As well as sharing the same [viceroy](/wiki/Lord_Lieutenant_of_Ireland "Lord Lieutenant of Ireland"), a [Council of Ireland](/wiki/Council_of_Ireland "Council of Ireland") was to be composed of 20 members from each parliament. The council would co-ordinate matters of common concern to the two parliaments (transport, health, agriculture etc.).[[22]](#cite_note-23) Each parliament was to possess the ability, in identical motions, to vote powers to the council, which Britain intended should evolve into a single Irish Parliament.[[23]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEThompson200143-24)[[24]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPeled201316-25) The council had little real power – the provisions of the act called for the council to promote consultations which could lead to eventual unity.[[25]](#cite_note-26) The Council never met.

## Aftermath

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Government_of_Ireland_Act_1920&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Aftermath")]

### Northern Ireland

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Government_of_Ireland_Act_1920&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Northern Ireland")]

The [Parliament of Northern Ireland](/wiki/Parliament_of_Northern_Ireland "Parliament of Northern Ireland") came into being in June 1921. Northern Ireland itself is considered to have been established on 3 May 1921 - the date on which the Government of Ireland Act came into force.[[26]](#cite_note-27) The first meetings of the Parliament of Northern Ireland were held at Belfast City Hall.[[27]](#cite_note-28) At its inauguration, in [Belfast City Hall](/wiki/Belfast_City_Hall "Belfast City Hall"), King [George V](/wiki/George_V "George V") made a famous appeal for Anglo-Irish and north–south reconciliation. The speech, drafted by the government of [David Lloyd George](/wiki/David_Lloyd_George "David Lloyd George") on recommendations from [Jan Smuts](/wiki/Jan_Smuts "Jan Smuts")[[b]](#cite_note-29) Prime Minister of the [Union of South Africa](/wiki/Union_of_South_Africa "Union of South Africa"), with the enthusiastic backing of the King, opened the door for formal contact between the [British Government](/wiki/British_Government "British Government") and the [Republican](/wiki/Irish_republicanism "Irish republicanism") administration of [Éamon de Valera](/wiki/%C3%89amon_de_Valera "Éamon de Valera").

Though it was superseded in large part, its repeal remained a matter of controversy until accomplished in the 1990s (under the provisions of the 1998 [Good Friday Agreement](/wiki/Good_Friday_Agreement "Good Friday Agreement")).[[6]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJackson2004368–370-6)

### Southern Ireland

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Government_of_Ireland_Act_1920&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Southern Ireland")]

All 128 MPs elected to the [House of Commons of Southern Ireland](/wiki/House_of_Commons_of_Southern_Ireland "House of Commons of Southern Ireland") in the [May 1921 elections](/wiki/1921_Northern_Ireland_and_Southern_Ireland_general_elections "1921 Northern Ireland and Southern Ireland general elections") were returned unopposed; 124 of them, representing [Sinn Féin](/wiki/Sinn_F%C3%A9in "Sinn Féin"), declared themselves [TDs](/wiki/Teachta_D%C3%A1la "Teachta Dála") (*Teachtaí Dála*, [Irish](/wiki/Irish_language "Irish language") for *Dáil Deputies*) and assembled as the [Second Dáil](/wiki/Second_D%C3%A1il "Second Dáil") of the [Irish Republic](/wiki/Irish_Republic "Irish Republic").

With only the four [Independent Unionist](/wiki/Independent_Unionist "Independent Unionist") MPs, who had been elected for the [Dublin University](/wiki/Dublin_University_(constituency) "Dublin University (constituency)") constituency and fifteen appointed senators turning up for the state opening of the [Parliament of Southern Ireland](/wiki/Parliament_of_Southern_Ireland "Parliament of Southern Ireland") at the Royal College of Science in Dublin (now [Government Buildings](/wiki/Government_Buildings "Government Buildings")) in June 1921, the new legislature was suspended. Southern Ireland was ruled, for the time being, directly from London as it had been before the Government of Ireland Act.

The [Provisional Government of the Irish Free State](/wiki/Provisional_Government_of_the_Irish_Free_State "Provisional Government of the Irish Free State") was constituted on 14 January 1922 "at a meeting of members of the Parliament elected for constituencies in Southern Ireland". That meeting was not convened as a meeting of the [House of Commons of Southern Ireland](/wiki/House_of_Commons_of_Southern_Ireland "House of Commons of Southern Ireland") nor as a meeting of the [Dáil](/wiki/D%C3%A1il "Dáil"). Instead, it was convened by [Arthur Griffith](/wiki/Arthur_Griffith "Arthur Griffith") as "Chairman of the Irish Delegation of Plenipotentiaries" (who had signed the [Anglo-Irish Treaty](/wiki/Anglo-Irish_Treaty "Anglo-Irish Treaty")) under the terms of the Treaty.[[c]](#cite_note-30)

[Elections in June 1922](/wiki/1922_Irish_general_election "1922 Irish general election") were followed by the meeting of the [Third Dáil](/wiki/Third_D%C3%A1il "Third Dáil"), which worked as a [Constituent Assembly](/wiki/Constituent_Assembly "Constituent Assembly") to draft a [constitution](/wiki/Constitution_of_the_Irish_Free_State "Constitution of the Irish Free State") for the [Irish Free State](/wiki/Irish_Free_State "Irish Free State"). For the purposes of British law the constitution was confirmed by the [Irish Free State Constitution Act 1922](/wiki/Irish_Free_State_Constitution_Act_1922 "Irish Free State Constitution Act 1922"); the new state then came into being on 6 December 1922.

### Consequences

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Government_of_Ireland_Act_1920&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Consequences")]

The Treaty provided for the ability of Northern Ireland's Parliament, by formal address, to opt out of the new [Irish Free State](/wiki/Irish_Free_State "Irish Free State"), which as expected, the [Parliament of Northern Ireland](/wiki/Parliament_of_Northern_Ireland "Parliament of Northern Ireland") brought into effect on 7 December 1922 (the day after the establishment of the Irish Free State). An [Irish Boundary Commission](/wiki/Irish_Boundary_Commission "Irish Boundary Commission") was set up to redraw the border between the new [Irish Free State](/wiki/Irish_Free_State "Irish Free State") and Northern Ireland, but it remained unchanged in return for financial concessions and the British and Irish governments agreed to suppress its report. In regards to the possible loss of territory due to the Boundary Commission's findings, on 25 January 1922 [James Craig](/wiki/James_Craig,_1st_Viscount_Craigavon "James Craig, 1st Viscount Craigavon"), the 1st [Prime Minister of Northern Ireland](/wiki/Prime_Minister_of_Northern_Ireland "Prime Minister of Northern Ireland") stated: ""I will never give in to any re-arrangement of the boundary that leaves our Ulster area less than it is under the Government of Ireland Act"[[28]](#cite_note-31) The newly formed Government of Northern Ireland refused to appoint a representative to the Boundary Commission. The [Council of Ireland](/wiki/Council_of_Ireland "Council of Ireland") never functioned as hoped (as an embryonic all-Ireland parliament), as the new governments decided to find a better mechanism in January 1922.[[29]](#cite_note-32)

In consequence of the establishment of the Irish Free State, the British parliament passed the [Irish Free State (Consequential Provisions) Act 1922](/wiki/Irish_Free_State_(Consequential_Provisions)_Act_1922 "Irish Free State (Consequential Provisions) Act 1922"), which made a number of adjustments to Northern Ireland's system of government as set up by the 1920 act. Most notably, the office of Lord Lieutenant was abolished, being replaced by the new office of [Governor of Northern Ireland](/wiki/Governor_of_Northern_Ireland "Governor of Northern Ireland").

## Repeal

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Government_of_Ireland_Act_1920&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Repeal")]

The final provisions of the 1920 Act remaining in force in the United Kingdom were repealed under the terms of the [Northern Ireland Act 1998](/wiki/Northern_Ireland_Act_1998 "Northern Ireland Act 1998"), after the [Good Friday Agreement](/wiki/Good_Friday_Agreement "Good Friday Agreement"). In the republic, the [Statute Law Revision Act 2007](/wiki/Statute_Law_Revision_Act_2007 "Statute Law Revision Act 2007") repealed the Act almost 85 years after [Constitution of the Irish Free State](/wiki/Constitution_of_the_Irish_Free_State "Constitution of the Irish Free State") replaced it as the basic constitutional law.[[30]](#cite_note-33)

After the war he decided that his future and that of South Africa lay in reconciliation between [Afrikaner](/wiki/Afrikaner "Afrikaner") and the British. In 1914 at the start of [World War I](/wiki/World_War_I "World War I") the Boer "bitter enders" rose against the government in the [Boer Revolt](/wiki/Boer_Revolt "Boer Revolt") and allied themselves with their old supporter Germany. General Smuts played an important part in crushing the rebellion and defeating the Germans in Africa, before fighting on the [Western Front](/wiki/Western_Front_(World_War_I) "Western Front (World War I)"). The South African establishment, of which Smuts was a part, in contrast to the British establishment in 1916, was lenient to the leaders of the revolt, who were fined and spent two years in prison. After this revolt and lenient treatment the "bitter enders" contented themselves with working within the system.
